exercises.title: Why Flash Attention isn't enough
exercises.prompt: Flash Attention made attention memory linear, so why do we still need sliding-window/sparse attention variants? Distinguish what Flash Attention changes from what it doesn't, in terms of memory vs arithmetic cost.
exercises.hint: Flash hides the O(N²) activation MEMORY but every token still attends to every other token → arithmetic stays O(N²). Variants change the attention topology to cut the actual compute.
exercises.solution:
**What Flash Attention fixes:** the **O(N²) activation memory**. By tiling and using the online softmax, it never materializes the full N×N score matrix in HBM, so attention's *memory footprint* becomes **linear (O(N))**. That's what made 128K-context training/inference fit in memory.

**What Flash Attention does NOT fix:** the **arithmetic**. It computes the *exact same* attention — **every token still attends to every other token**, so the compute is still **O(N²)** in sequence length. Flash makes those O(N²) FLOPs *fast and memory-cheap*, but it doesn't *reduce their number*.

**Why that still hurts:** for very long context the quadratic *compute* itself becomes the wall. A 128K-context, 70B model has ~16 billion attention entries **per layer × 80 layers** — even at peak FLOPs that's enormous, and it grows quadratically as context extends. 

**So the variants attack a different axis:** instead of computing full attention efficiently, they **change the topology of the attention matrix** — each token attends to *fewer* tokens (a local window, a sparse pattern, etc.), cutting the *arithmetic* from O(N²) to roughly **O(N·W)**. Flash Attention (memory-efficient *full* attention) and attention variants (*less* attention) are **complementary**: production long-context models use both — sparse/windowed patterns *implemented with* Flash-style kernels.

exercises.title: Implement sliding-window (local) attention
exercises.prompt: Implement causal sliding-window attention where each token attends only to itself and the previous W-1 tokens. Show the masked attention pattern (a banded lower triangle), state the memory/compute complexity, and explain how stacking layers still gives a large effective receptive field.
exercises.hint: Mask out positions j where j>i (causal) OR i-j>=W (too far back). Complexity O(N·W). Like CNN receptive fields: L layers of window W reach ~L·W back.
exercises.solution:
```python
import numpy as np

def softmax(z, axis=-1):
    z = z - z.max(axis=axis, keepdims=True); e = np.exp(z)
    return e / e.sum(axis=axis, keepdims=True)

def sliding_window_attention(Q, K, V, W):
    """Causal local attention: token i sees [i-W+1 .. i]. Q,K,V: (T, d)."""
    T, d = Q.shape
    i = np.arange(T)[:, None]; j = np.arange(T)[None, :]
    allowed = (j <= i) & (i - j < W)          # causal AND within window
    scores = (Q @ K.T) / np.sqrt(d)
    scores = np.where(allowed, scores, -np.inf)
    attn = softmax(scores, axis=-1)
    return attn @ V, attn

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
T, d, W = 10, 8, 3
Q, K, Vv = (rng.standard_normal((T, d)) for _ in range(3))
_, attn = sliding_window_attention(Q, K, Vv, W)
print(np.round(attn, 2))   # banded lower-triangular: each row has <= W nonzeros
```
The mask keeps only a **band** of the lower triangle — each row `i` has at most `W` non-zero weights (columns `i-W+1 .. i`).

**Complexity:** instead of O(N²), each token does O(W) work → **O(N·W) compute and memory** for the attention. With `W` fixed (e.g. 4096) and N large (128K), that's a linear-in-N cost — a big saving. (Real kernels compute only the band, not the full matrix then mask.)

**Why stacking recovers a large receptive field — the CNN analogy:** a single SWA layer only sees `W` tokens back, but **information propagates across layers**. Layer 1 lets token `i` see `i-W+1..i`; layer 2 lets it see the *representations* of those tokens, which already absorbed *their* windows — so after `L` layers the **effective receptive field is ~`L·W`** tokens (exactly like stacking convolutions grows the receptive field). A 32-layer model with W=4096 can route information across ~128K tokens, even though no single layer attends globally. Models like **Mistral 7B** and **Gemma 2/3** use SWA (often **interleaved** with occasional full-attention layers) to keep most layers cheap while a few global layers guarantee true long-range mixing.

exercises.title: The variant zoo: sparse, sliding-window, and differential attention
exercises.prompt: Compare the families of attention variants — full, sliding-window, sparse/dilated (and global-token hybrids like Longformer/BigBird), and differential attention — on what each changes, its complexity, and its failure mode or tradeoff. When would you reach for each, and why do many production models interleave full and local layers rather than going fully sparse?
exercises.hint: Sparse/dilated patterns + a few global tokens approximate full attention at O(N·k). Differential attention subtracts two softmax maps to cancel attention noise (improves long-context retrieval). Pure local loses true long-range; hence interleaving.
exercises.solution:
| Variant | What it changes | Complexity | Tradeoff / failure mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full attention** | every token attends to all | O(N²) compute, O(N) mem with Flash | exact but quadratic compute; the long-context wall |
| **Sliding-window (SWA)** | only a local band of W neighbors | O(N·W) | no *direct* long-range link in one layer; relies on depth to reach far (Gemma, Mistral) |
| **Sparse / dilated + global tokens** (Longformer, BigBird) | fixed sparse pattern: local window + **strided/dilated** jumps + a few **global tokens** that attend everywhere | O(N·k) | approximation of full attention; pattern is hand-designed and may miss some dependencies; global tokens add a small dense piece |
| **Differential attention** | computes **two** softmax attention maps and **subtracts** them (like a differential amplifier) | ~same as full (two maps) | aims to **cancel attention 'noise'** (spurious attention to irrelevant tokens), improving long-context **retrieval/needle-in-haystack** and reducing hallucination; more compute per layer, newer/less battle-tested |

**The shared idea for the sparse family:** you don't need *every* pairwise interaction. **Local windows** capture short-range structure, a handful of **global tokens** (e.g. `[CLS]`, task tokens) give every position an indirect long-range hop, and **dilation/strides** spread coverage — together approximating full attention at near-linear cost. BigBird even has theoretical guarantees that local+global+random ≈ full for many tasks.

**Differential attention is a different goal:** not cheaper, but *cleaner*. Standard softmax attention assigns small but nonzero weight to lots of irrelevant tokens ('attention noise'), which dilutes signal in very long contexts. Subtracting two attention maps cancels the common-mode noise and **sharpens focus on relevant tokens** — measurably better long-context retrieval and less distraction.

**When to reach for each:**
- **Short/medium context, need exactness:** full attention (with Flash). Don't over-engineer.
- **Very long context, throughput-bound:** SWA or sparse/dilated to kill the quadratic compute.
- **Long-context where *retrieval accuracy* matters (RAG, long docs):** differential attention or generous global tokens.

**Why interleave full + local instead of going fully sparse:** pure local/sparse attention has a **real failure mode** — some tasks need a genuine, *direct* long-range dependency that a windowed layer can only reach after many layers (and depth-limited models may not propagate it faithfully), causing degradation on long-range reasoning and exact retrieval. So production models (Gemma 2/3, GPT-OSS-style) **interleave**: most layers are cheap **local** (W-window) while **every few layers is full (global)** attention. You get ~linear average cost *and* guaranteed true long-range mixing at the global layers — the pragmatic best-of-both that beats committing to either extreme.
